ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for flower tea or floral tea blend; closest parent 'tea (food product)' used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0AN (Tea and tea-based beverages) is the closest defensible parent category; no specific code for mixed flower tea exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 'Tea (substance)' used as closest parent; no specific concept for flower tea blend exists.

main_ingredients: dried flowers (e.g. chrysanthemum, rose, jasmine, osmanthus, lavender)

serving.natural_language_portion: One pot of flower tea served at a Chinese restaurant, typically yielding approximately 500 ml of brewed floral infusion, equivalent to roughly 3–4 cups.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, tea, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 14355)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Flower tea (百花茶) is a blend of dried edible flowers steeped in hot water. No specific entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN for this beverage. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain brewed tea (NDB 14355 / FDC 171917) is used as the closest defensible analogue. Plain brewed tea and plain flower tea share essentially identical macronutrient profiles: negligible energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate. Sodium and potassium values are adapted from the USDA entry. Micronutrients not reported by the source are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC)
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Flower tea contains negligible digestible carbohydrates (approximately 0.3 g per 100 ml). Plain brewed tea and herbal infusions are classified as having a negligible glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database lists plain tea and herbal infusions as effectively zero GI. Per Veritas convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 500 ml serving is 0.0 given the trace carbohydrate content.

hypertension.guidance: Flower tea contains approximately 3 mg sodium per 100 ml and is essentially sodium-free, making it a suitable beverage choice for individuals managing hypertension; unsweetened consumption is recommended.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Flower tea provides approximately 0.3 g carbohydrate per 100 ml with a negligible glycemic load, making it a safe beverage for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, and antioxidant phytochemicals from dried flowers
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Flower tea is free of fat, saturated fat, and cholesterol, and dried flowers such as chrysanthemum and rose contribute antioxidant flavonoids that may support cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flower tea (百花茶, bǎi huā chá) is a traditional Chinese herbal infusion made by steeping a blend of dried edible flowers — commonly chrysanthemum (菊花), rose (玫瑰), jasmine (茉莉), osmanthus (桂花), and lavender — in hot water. In Singapore's Chinese restaurant context, it is typically served by the pot (壶), yielding approximately 500 ml of lightly coloured, fragrant infusion. The beverage is caffeine-free or very low in caffeine depending on the flower blend, and is prized for its delicate floral aroma, mild sweetness, and soothing properties. Nutritionally, flower tea is essentially calorie-free: it contains negligible protein, fat, and carbohydrate, with energy of approximately 1 kcal per 100 ml. Sodium content is minimal at around 3 mg per 100 ml. The glycemic index is effectively zero, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. Traditional Chinese medicine attributes cooling and detoxifying properties to chrysanthemum-based flower teas. Clinically, unsweetened flower tea is appropriate for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. The pot format served at restaurants such as See Ya Tonight (今晚有局) in Singapore typically serves 3–4 people and is priced at approximately SGD 23.40.
common_user_queries: Is flower tea good for diabetics?
How many calories in flower tea?
What flowers are in 百花茶?
Is flower tea suitable for hypertension?
What does flower tea taste like?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for flower tea (百花茶) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy plain brewed tea (FDC 171917), which is the closest defensible analogue given that flower tea is an aqueous infusion of plant material with negligible macronutrient content. The adaptation is well-supported by the nature of the food. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact flower blend composition is not standardised and minor variation in micronutrient content is possible.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero: flower tea is an aqueous infusion of dried flowers with no protein contribution detectable above trace levels; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central brewed tea analogue. 2. fat_g is naturally zero: no lipid-contributing ingredients are present in a plain flower tea infusion; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central brewed tea analogue. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are naturally zero for the same reason. 4. sugar_g is naturally zero for unsweetened flower tea; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central brewed tea analogue. 5. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as the flowers are removed after steeping and the liquid infusion contains no measurable fibre. 6. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per Veritas convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 per 500 ml serving. 7. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for brewed tea. 8. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 9. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 10. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 11. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 12. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 13. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 14. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 15. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 16. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 17. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 18. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 19. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 20. Serving size set to 500 g (ml) representing a standard restaurant pot. Price from field survey data (SGD 23.40) is noted for context but not used as a nutritional source. 21. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301103 is used as closest parent for tea food product; no specific flower tea term exists in FoodOn as of April 2026.

Documented all -1 sentinel values with explicit source-not-reported reasons in notes.
